envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Wet Corn Milling and Starch Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring B2B ingredient shipments under spot, annual, and multi-year contracts; pricing may be fixed or fee-based with raw-material adjustments, and differentiation ranges from commodity sweeteners and native starches to higher-function specialty ingredients.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, dry corn milling, wet milling whose primary purpose is nonpotable ethyl alcohol, purchased-oil refining, shell entities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent merchant wet corn milling and starch plants selling recurring volumes of starches, glucose, dextrose, fructose, corn oil, feed co-products, and related functional ingredients to external food, beverage, paper, packaging, pharmaceutical, personal-care, and industrial customers.

a.bridge: BLS food-processing equipment work and the Corn Refiners Association process description proxy the 311221 task and wage mix. I map production records, process surveillance, scheduling, quality documentation, maintenance triage, procurement, and customer/specification work to AI exposure while excluding most physical receiving, cleaning, separation, drying, maintenance, and safety work. The proxy likely overstates not-yet-automated exposure because the industry already uses central process-control computers.
caveats: No public wage-weighted 311221 task inventory was located.
Trade-association descriptions may reflect larger and more automated member plants than LMM firms.
a.change_evidence: A 311221-specific OEWS staffing and wage table plus plant time studies by function that separate existing DCS, laboratory, ERP, and robotic automation from remaining manual work.
a.rationale: Wet milling is continuous, capital-intensive physical processing. Information tasks around control rooms, laboratories, maintenance, logistics, contracts, and technical service are AI-assistable, but central process control is already established and much remaining plant labor is physical, regulated, or safety-critical.

d5.bridge: USDA reports U.S. corn-sweetener availability falling from 85.7 pounds per person in 1999 to 53.0 in 2023, while CRA and Ingredion document broad starch, oil, feed, food, packaging, pharmaceutical, personal-care, and industrial uses. I translate the persistent sweetener decline plus diversified non-sweetener uses into a near-flat five-year constant-quality basket. The low case assumes sweetener and processed-food pressure dominates; the high case assumes specialty starch, biomaterials, packaging, industrial, and fermentation applications gain mix and volume.
caveats: Per-capita food availability is not producer shipment volume and covers only corn sweeteners.
Public-company product breadth may exceed that of LMM plants.
d5.change_evidence: USDA and CRA shipment series split by HFCS, glucose, dextrose, native and modified starch, feed, oil, biomaterials, and end market, combined with customer reformulation forecasts and announced capacity through 2031.
d5.rationale: HFCS and other caloric sweeteners face a structural consumption headwind, but the industry's service basket is much broader than sweeteners. Starch functionality, feed co-products, corn oil, industrial binders, packaging and paper, personal care, pharmaceuticals, and newer biomaterials provide offsetting demand.

caveats: The frozen estimate of 17 LMM-band firms is margin-bridged rather than observed and may be strained in a capital-intensive concentrated industry.
A large public producer's structure may not represent hypothetical LMM operators.
e.change_evidence: An establishment-to-firm census for 311221 containing ownership, EBITDA, merchant versus captive output, customer concentration, shared infrastructure, environmental reserves, and control-transfer constraints.
e.rationale: A standalone merchant plant with recurring external customers, separable assets, compliant operations, and diversified products can qualify. Eligibility is reduced by captive or vertically integrated ownership, very large strategic plants, environmental or process-safety liabilities, commodity-only economics, customer concentration, and dependence on shared infrastructure.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 311221 ($1,656,669,356) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 311221 ($17,655,099,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $2,288,412 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 311221); class <5: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,342,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $357,231 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,125,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,086,613 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,458,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,357,396 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $28,443,875 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,337,691 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $200,301,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,545,902 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,787,243,833 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $425,054,685 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 17

caveats: This is structural judgment, not a measured retention rate.
Demand volume changes are captured in d5 rather than counted again here.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy surveys, announced captive wet-milling projects, import penetration, substitution studies, and end-user qualification data for alternative starches and sweeteners.
o.rationale: Software cannot replace the physical steeping, separation, conversion, drying, testing, storage, and transport required to turn corn into starches, sweeteners, oil, and feed co-products. AI can reduce labor intensity but not eliminate accountable plant operation; only customer self-supply, vertical integration, imports, or material substitution removes the merchant operator.

q.bridge: Ingredion's 2025 filing describes U.S./Canada starch and sweetener competition based on functionality, price, and quality; contracts typically last one year or less, one-third of sales are multi-year fee-based, and 2025 net sales reflected pass-through of lower corn costs. I use this large-producer evidence to infer substantial renewal and formula pass-through for LMM firms. It may understate retention for differentiated specialty producers and overstate it where a smaller supplier lacks bargaining power.
caveats: Ingredion is much larger and more diversified than the lens firms.
The filing does not isolate the retained share of AI-enabled benefits.
q.change_evidence: Target contract and invoice cohorts showing formula terms, fee versus fixed pricing, renewal cadence, rebates, bid frequency, product-level gross margins, and realized repricing after documented productivity changes.
q.rationale: Input-indexed and short-duration contracts, close substitutes, and price competition return much of a general cost reduction to customers. Operators can retain more from proprietary functionality, yield, reliability, technical service, and differentiated specialty formulations than from commodity starch or sweetener output.

caveats: The range is implementation judgment rather than an observed adoption rate.
Brownfield control systems and cybersecurity posture may vary materially across facilities.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level pilots with audited hours saved, false-positive rates, yield and downtime impacts, validation protocols, operator acceptance, and deployment costs across at least three wet mills.
rho.rationale: Within five years, documentation copilots, computer-vision inspection, predictive-maintenance triage, planning, and control-room decision support can be implemented on top of existing digital systems. Validation, cyber and process-safety requirements, food-quality accountability, legacy integration, and the cost of errors keep implementation below the exposed ceiling.

s5.bridge: Gallup found 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I reduce the center for a capital-intensive processing niche where strategic, corporate, joint-venture, or family ownership and plant interdependence can make a transaction non-qualifying. The survey may overstate qualifying control transfers because it includes gifts and plans, but may omit unplanned strategic sales.
caveats: The source covers all employer businesses, not this industry or EBITDA band.
Planned transfers are not completed deals and can include non-sale gifts.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, and state-registry review of every U.S. starch or wet-milling control transaction since 2016, plus owner-age and succession interviews for independent LMM operators.
s5.rationale: The Gallup result is a useful five-year anchor but not a 311221 transaction measure. A wide interval reflects the small target universe and uncertainty over whether transfers deliver standalone operating control.

principal_driver: Diverse physical ingredient uses preserve operator-required demand beyond declining corn sweeteners.
principal_weakness: Existing automation and formula-driven competitive pricing limit both incremental labor opportunity and retained benefit.
